query: recent clinical trials on baricitinib effectiveness in rheumatoid arthritis

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Baricitinib is a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) indicated for rheumatoid arthritis when conventional DMARDs prove inadequate.

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors Baricitinib is an oral selective inhibitor of Janus kinase (JAK) 1 and JAK 2 currently indicated for moderate to severely active rheumatoid arthritis patients. Baricitinib was considered a potential treatment for COVID-19 based on its inhibitory effect on SARS-CoV-2 endocytosis in vitro and on the intracellular signaling pathway of cytokines that cause the late-onset hyperinflammatory state that results in severe illness. [115] [116] This dual inhibitory effect makes it a promising therapeutic drug against all stages of COVID-19. A multicenter observational, retrospective study of 113 hospitalized patients with COVID-19 pneumonia who received baricitinib combined with lopinavir/ritonavir (baricitinib arm, n=113) or hydroxychloroquine and lopinavir/ritonavir (control arm, n=78) reported significant improvement in clinical symptoms and 2-week mortality rate in the baricitinib arm compared with the control arm. Results from the ACTT-2 trial, a double-blind, randomized placebo-controlled trial evaluating baricitinib plus remdesivir in hospitalized adult patients with COVID-19, reported that the combination therapy of baricitinib plus remdesivir was superior to remdesivir therapy alone in not only reducing recovery time but also accelerating clinical improvement in hospitalized patients with COVID-19, particularly who were receiving high flow oxygen supplementation or non-invasive ventilation. [117] Baricitinib, in combination with remdesivir, has been approved for clinical use in hospitalized patients with COVID-19 under a EUA issued by the FDA. The efficacy of baricitinib alone or in combination with remdesivir has not been evaluated in the SARS-CoV-2 variants, and there is limited data on the use of baricitinib with dexamethasone. Ruxolitinib is another oral selective inhibitor of JAK 1 and 2 indicated for myeloproliferative disorders, polycythemia vera, and steroid-resistant GVHD. Similar to baricitinib, it has been hypothesized to have an inhibitory effect on cytokines' intracellular signaling pathway, making it a potential treatment against COVID-19. Results from a small prospective multicenter randomized controlled phase 2 trial evaluating the efficacy and safety of ruxolitinib reported no statistical difference from the standard of care. However, most patients demonstrated significant chest CT improvement and faster recovery from lymphopenia. [118] A large randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled multicenter trial (NCT04362137) is ongoing to assess ruxolitinib's efficacy and safety in patients with severe COVID-19. Tofacitinib is another oral selective inhibitor of JAK 1 and JAK3 that is indicated for moderate to severe RA, psoriatic arthritis, and moderate to severe ulcerative colitis. Given its inhibitory effect on the inflammatory cascade, it was hypothesized that its use could ameliorate the viral inflammation-mediated lung injury in patients with severe COVID-19. Results from a small randomized controlled trial that evaluated the efficacy involving 289 patients who were randomized to receive tofacitinib or placebo showed that tofacitinib led to a lower risk of respiratory failure or death (PMID:34133856).

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Baricitinib medication is approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to treat adult patients with moderately to severely active rheumatoid arthritis who have not responded adequately to other DMARDs, including tumor necrosis factor (TNF) antagonist therapies. Several clinical trials have demonstrated significant clinical improvement and inhibition of the disease's progression compared to a placebo. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Function -- Anti-inflammatory properties of Baricitinib
retrieved_docs.content: Baricitinib is a selective and reversible inhibitor of Janus Kinases (JAK) JAK1 and JAK2 originally approved for use in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis (RA). Its use has been justified by proven dampening of the inflammatory response [5] . The JAK/STAT pathway has been identified as one of the subcellular pathways that mediate signal transduction of external stimuli to the nuclei of the cell, thereby mediating normal as well as aberrant immune responses. When cytokines attach to their receptors on the surface of the cell, a conformational change in the receptor leads to activation of the associated JAK complex. Once activated, the JAK complex autophosphorylates, creating a docking site for signaling molecules of the STAT family. The activated JAK complex also shows increased Janus Kinase activity leading to further phosphorylation of the STAT signaling pathway. The phosphorylated STAT molecules are then released from the receptor, translocate to the nucleus and lead to DNA sequence activation. This ultimately leads to target gene transcription [6] . The JAK signaling pathway family includes 4 proteins, the JAK1, JAK2, JAK3, and tyrosine kinase 2 (TYK2) [7] . Disruption of one or more of the JAK proteins is associated with an interruption in the signal transduction pathway and can lead to the beneficial effects of dampening a heightened immune response [8] . Cytokines implicated in causing the cytokine storm like the IFN-γ, IL-6, etc. have a JAK-mediated mechanism of action. IL6 is associated with JAK1, JAK2, and TYK2 [9] , whereas IFN-γ activity is mediated by JAK1/JAK2 [10] . Stebbing and colleagues validated the above-mentioned mechanism in their study. On evaluating the in-vitro pharmacology in relevant leucocytes as well as in-vivo pharmacokinetics of Baricitinib, they were able to demonstrate inhibition of IL-2, IL-6, IL-10, IFN-γ, and GM-CSF [11] . See Figure. Baricitinib Mechanism of Action

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatoid arthritis: Baricitinib is recommended at a once-daily oral dosage of 2 mg, with or without food. The drug can be used either as a monotherapy or in combination with non-biologic DMARDs or methotrexate.

retrieved_docs.title: Upadacitinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Upadacitinib is a new FDA-approved second-line agent for treating moderate to severe active rheumatoid arthritis (RA) in patients who have not shown an adequate response or intolerance to the first-line agent, methotrexate. [1] This agent is a second-generation selective Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitor targeting the JAK1 enzyme. [2] Upadacitinib received FDA approval on August 16, 2019, based on positive and promising results from its multinational phase III trials in subjects with moderate to severe rheumatoid arthritis. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the appropriate patient population for baricitinib therapy, recognizing those with moderate-to-severe active rheumatoid arthritis who have not responded adequately to other disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs. Differentiate between the indications for baricitinib in rheumatoid arthritis and its FDA-approved use in the treatment of COVID-19. Assess patients regularly for potential adverse effects, specifically on laboratory monitoring, including liver function tests, lipid panels, and complete blood counts. Collaborate with an interprofessional healthcare team, including specialists, pharmacists, and nurses, to improve treatment outcomes and ensure comprehensive and coordinated care for patients on baricitinib. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Baricitinib is a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) indicated for rheumatoid arthritis when conventional DMARDs prove inadequate. This medication is approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to treat adult patients with moderately to severely active rheumatoid arthritis who have not responded adequately to other DMARDs, including tumor necrosis factor antagonist therapies. Furthermore, baricitinib is FDA-approved for treating COVID-19, presenting a valuable choice in combination with other therapies for hospitalized patients, including pediatric cases necessitating supplemental oxygen, invasive mechanical ventilation, or extracorporeal membrane oxygenation. This activity comprehensively reviews the indications, contraindications, mechanisms, adverse events, and other crucial aspects of baricitinib therapy within the clinical setting. This activity is tailored to meet the specific needs of an interprofessional healthcare team responsible for treating patients with rheumatoid arthritis, alopecia areata, and COVID-19.

retrieved_docs.title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Baricitinib is a medication traditionally used in the management and treatment of Rheumatoid Arthritis. However, with the recent advent of the COVID-19 global pandemic, several agents have been evaluated for the management of this disease. Baricitinib is in the JAK inhibitor class of medications. This activity reviews the indications, action, and contraindications for the concurrent use of Remdesivir and Baricitinib as valuable agents in the management of COVID-19. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors like indications and contraindications, dosing, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, pertinent for members of the interprofessional team in the management of patients with COVID-19 and related conditions

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) typically used in treating RA include methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, sulfasalazine, and leflunomide. Anti-TNF-alpha inhibitors include etanercept, infliximab, adalimumab, golimumab, and certolizumab pegol. Other biologic DMARDs include interleukin (IL) 6 inhibitors such as tocilizumab and sarilumab, T-cell costimulation inhibitors such as abatacept (CTLA4-Ig), and the anti-CD20 B-cell depleting monoclonal antibody such as rituximab. Targeted synthetic DMARDs include Janus kinases (JAK) inhibitors such as tofacitinib, baricitinib, and upadacitinib. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Safe and Effective Use of Baricitinib and Remdesivir in Hospitalized Patients With Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Baricitinib has also been associated with a dose-dependent increased tendency to have venous as well as arterial thromboembolism, which is similar to other disease-modifying anti rheumatoid drugs (DMARD’s). It is for this reason that the FDA approved a 2 mg/day dose of Baricitinib for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis, as the benefit of administering a 4 mg/day dose was unclear with an increased risk of thromboembolic events [23] . COVID-19 is associated with immune modulated hypercoagulability and whether the immunosuppressive effects of Baricitinib will act as a positive influence, or if its tendency to cause thromboembolic events will increase the risk of clots remains to be evaluated. The largest study to date with COVID-19 is ACTT-2 and the incidence of adverse events was noted to be less than the placebo arm which continues to be reassuring.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- ACR and EULAR Treatment Guidelines
retrieved_docs.content: If still uncontrolled despite the above trials, use tofacitinib

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Contraindications -- Box Warnings
retrieved_docs.content: Mortality: Postmarketing studies demonstrate a higher all-cause mortality rate, including sudden cardiovascular death, in patients aged 50 or older with rheumatoid arthritis and at least one cardiovascular risk factor who are treated with baricitinib compared to those receiving TNF blockers.

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Administration -- Available Dosage Forms and Strengths
retrieved_docs.content: Baricitinib is administered orally and is available in 2 different strengths—2 mg and 4 mg. The recommended dosage for rheumatoid arthritis is 2 mg orally once daily. In the context of COVID-19 infection, 2 mg oral baricitinib was assessed in clinical trials alongside 10 to 14 days of antiviral therapy. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- Janus kinase (JAK) Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: JAK is a group of tyrosine kinases that participate in intracellular signal transduction for hematopoiesis and immune cell function. JAK inhibitors (such as tofacitinib) are oral agents that reduce the production of cytokines and are approved as second-line agents for the treatment of RA. [112]

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Tofacitinib's indication for active psoriatic arthritis received FDA approval following two phase III clinical trials, OPAL Broaden and OPAL Beyond. [3] The trials included subjects with psoriatic arthritis receiving tofacitinib treatment with a 5 mg dosage twice a day in sequence with MTX or other conventional synthetic DMARD therapy. Similar to RA, it is indicated for psoriatic arthritis in adult patients unresponsive or developed interlace to one or more disease-modifying rheumatoid drugs (DMARDs) with an active moderate to severe psoriatic disease course.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFLAMMATORY AND ANTIRHEUMATIC PRODUCTS represent a therapeutic class that includes both specific antirheumatic agents and formulations that combine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. By encompassing individual agents targeted at rheumatic diseases as well as combination products that provide synergistic anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic effects, this class offers a broad range of options for managing inflammatory and rheumatic conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products constitute a broad category of medications that includes antiinflammatory/antirheumatic agents used in combination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Fenylbutazon and oxyfenbutazon are both classified as members of the butylpyrazolidine group of anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. As butylpyrazolidine derivatives, these agents share a common chemical scaffold that confers anti‑inflammatory and disease‑modifying properties useful in the treatment of rheumatic disorders. Recognizing fenylbutazon and oxyfenbutazon as part of this drug class helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, and potential side‑effect profiles within the broader category of butylpyrazolidine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1729
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoarthritis is classified as إلتهاب العظم والمفصل, which translates to bone and joint inflammation. Valdecoxib may be used to treat إلتهاب العظم والمفصل, indicating that this medication has potential therapeutic value in managing the inflammatory aspects of osteoarthritis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and this class includes the drugs ibrutinib and zanubrutinib. Ibrutinib, as a member of the BTK inhibitor group, may be used to treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia). Similarly, Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are also classified within the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; this EGFR inhibitor group comprises agents such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. Together, BTK inhibitors and EGFR inhibitors illustrate how distinct targeted therapies share common mechanistic foundations as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulatory effects, underscoring their relevance in the treatment of various cancers and immune‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2674
retrieved_docs.content: Alergeny is described as having the structural class of auranofin, indicating that the chemical framework of alergeny is related to the gold‑containing compound auranofin. Recognizing this structural relationship helps clarify how alergeny may share pharmacologic properties, mechanisms of action, or potential cross‑reactivity with auranofin, which is used in the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis. Understanding such structural classifications is important for anticipating therapeutic effects, side‑effect profiles, and guiding patient‑specific medication decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Quinolines, antirheumatic drugs are classified as specific antirheumatic agents, indicating that this subgroup of quinoline compounds is used specifically to treat rheumatic conditions within the broader category of antirheumatic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1665
retrieved_docs.content: Auranofin 3 mg oral capsule is classified as a gold preparation, representing a member of the gold‑based drug class that is used therapeutically, particularly in the management of conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1762
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort is categorized under the therapeutic class Immunological Agents [TC], indicating that it functions as an immunomodulatory medication used to suppress immune activity in various clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this therapeutic class, zanubrutinib is a specific BTK inhibitor that exemplifies the targeted approach of these agents. By inhibiting BTK, these drugs are employed in the treatment of certain malignancies and immune‑mediated conditions, reflecting their role as both anticancer and immune‑modulating therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepirumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepirumab target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, while roflumilast is a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that modulates inflammatory cell activity. Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same ATC class helps clinicians understand their shared therapeutic purpose and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and comparative effectiveness assessments in the management of obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1739
retrieved_docs.content: Fish oil (rybí oleje) may be used as a therapeutic option for rheumatoid arthritis and related diseases, suggesting that its omega‑3 fatty acid content can help alleviate inflammatory symptoms associated with these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1366
retrieved_docs.content: Aspirin is frequently noted to co‑occur with digoxin, indicating that these two drugs are often prescribed together or encountered in the same patient populations, which may reflect overlapping therapeutic needs such as cardiovascular management alongside anti‑inflammatory treatment. In addition to its well‑known antiplatelet and analgesic effects, aspirin may also be used to treat rheumatoid arthritis and related inflammatory diseases, providing symptomatic relief through its anti‑inflammatory properties. Recognizing both the common concurrent use of aspirin with digoxin and its potential role in managing rheumatoid arthritis helps clinicians coordinate therapy, monitor for drug interactions, and optimize overall patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.
